graphic.name: slingshot
graphic.caption: Stand outside a circle and aim two secants in, like a slingshot. Each beam slices through, hitting a near point and a far point.

steps.body:
A secant is a line that slices straight through a circle, crossing it at two points. From a point P outside the circle, two secants reach in: the two far crossing points cup the [far arc](orange); the two near ones cup the [near arc](teal).
From outside, [the angle at P](magenta) is half the DIFFERENCE of those two arcs:
[angle](magenta) = ½ ([far arc](orange) − [near arc](teal)).
Here the [far arc](orange) is 120° and the [near arc](teal) is 40°, so the angle is ½ (120° − 40°) = 40°.

steps.prompt: Drag P and watch the two arcs
steps.subtitle: Compare [the angle at P](magenta) with ½ ([far arc](orange) − [near arc](teal)) in the readout as you drag.

steps.prompt: Pick every true statement about these arcs
steps.subtitle: Two secants leave the outside point P. The [far arc](orange) is 120° and the [near arc](teal) is 40°.

options.label: The [far arc](orange) is the bigger of the two.
The [near arc](teal) is the one tucked closest to P.
The [far arc](orange) lies between the two far crossing points.
The [near arc](teal) is bigger than the [far arc](orange).
To get [the angle at P](magenta), you add the two arcs.

feedback.correct: Right. The far arc is the larger one, and from outside the circle you subtract the arcs — never add.
feedback.incorrect: From outside, the far arc is the bigger one and the angle SUBTRACTS the arcs. Adding is for chords that cross inside.

stages: Two secants leave the outside point P. Each cuts the circle at a near point and a far point: near M and N, far A and B.
Draw the chord AN, joining the far point A to the near point N. Now look at triangle PAN.
∠ANB is an inscribed angle sitting on the far arc AB (120°), so it is half of that arc: 60°.
∠NAM is an inscribed angle sitting on the near arc MN (40°), so it is half of that arc: 20°.
∠ANB is the outside corner of triangle PAN, so ∠P = ∠ANB − ∠NAM = 60° − 20° = 40°, which is ½ (120° − 40°).

blanks.prompt: ∠ANB sits on the far arc, 120°. An inscribed angle is half its arc: ½ × 120 = ?
∠NAM sits on the near arc, 40°. Half its arc: ½ × 40 = ?

problems.prompt: Two secants from an external point cut off a far arc of 120° and a near arc of 40°. Find the angle at the point.

feedback.incorrect: Outside the circle, the angle is half the difference of the two arcs.

problems.prompt: Two secants from an external point cut off a far arc of 100° and a near arc of 30°. Find the angle at the point.

feedback.incorrect: Outside the circle, the angle is half the difference of the two arcs.

problems.prompt: The angle between two secants from an external point is 25°, and the far arc is 110°. Find the near arc.

feedback.correct: Correct. The far arc minus twice the angle: 110° − 2 × 25° = 60°.
feedback.incorrect: The angle is half (far − near), so near = far − 2 × angle.

steps.prompt: Work out the angle one move at a time
steps.intro: Two secants from an external point cut off a [far arc](orange) of 160° and a [near arc](teal) of 70°. Find [the angle at the point](magenta) — subtract the near arc from the far arc, then halve.
steps.subtitle: Outside the circle: [angle](magenta) = ½ ([far arc](orange) − [near arc](teal)).

parts.prompt: First, subtract the near arc from the far arc: 160° − 70° = ?
parts.hint: Take the near arc away from the far arc: 160 − 70.
parts.note: 160° − 70° = 90° — the difference of the two arcs.

parts.prompt: Now halve that difference to get the angle at P: ½ × 90° = ?
parts.note: ½ × 90° = 45°, so the angle at P is 45°.

steps.doneNote: That's the whole method: subtract the near arc from the far arc, then take half. ½ (160° − 70°) = 45°.
